cided by the judges to not come un.
der the law, but that grand jury
investigation could report them and
likely put a stop to them through
publicity. They asked us this ques.
ton: which do you deem most
important, the prosecution of public
officials for crimes under the law
Implying that they had little faith
in the outcome of such prosecu-
tions) or the investigation and re-
port on serious misconduct in public
life" We promptly replied that I'M
der the circumstances we believed
an investigation by grand jury to
cure these immoralities and irreg-
ularities was more important than
any criminal charges against Off
Gals. The judges expressed them
selves that these charges were most
serious and needed correction-one
of them being that prisoners in the
county jail were denied treatment
in the hospital section of the county
Jail because jailors used the place
for sexual intercourse with female
prisoners-and yet they now tell us
that the Showing was insufficient"


Your committee has no recon-
mendation to make in the matter, in
the way of outlining further action
by this Central Labor council in ap-
pealing to the authorities of city
and county government for cleaner
and better government. We have
appealed to the city council and our
demand for an honest investigation
of corruption in the police depart
ment, with representation on such
investigation committee, chosen by
this body, and were ignored. We
have appealed to the superior
judges to give us the opportunity of
getting at the matters through a
grand jury, and here again we have
met with refusal. We have no con-
fidence in the honesty of purpose of
the county prosecuting attorney in
prosecuting criminal charges
against city and county officials, as
his action in the Elliott case will
show, inasmuch as he has not even
yet brought chare of betrayal and
abortion against Officer Glider,
though he has confessions of the
victim herself in the matter So in
the way of the regularly constituted
authorities there seems no way of
getting at the sources of public cor
ruption, immoralities and irregu-
larities which are know nto exist
and of which your committee Of
fered plenty of evidence before the
superior judges. Publicity seems to
be the only way open, and how this
can be had to any great extent,
with only the columns of The Spo-
kane Press and the Labor World
open, is a. problem that will be left
to you to determine. Respectfully
submitted, General Committee of
Fifteen.
